Explore the codebase to understand:
1. What ingestion providers exist (Docker Hub, GitHub, PyPI, etc.)
2. How sources are currently added and configured
3. The source data model/schema
4. How the polling/crawling works

Focus on internal/ingestion/ and any related database models. Report a concise summary.

content.content:
1ŌåÆ# Architecture: Changelogue
     2ŌåÆ
     3ŌåÆ## Overview
     4ŌåÆ
     5ŌåÆChangelogue is an event-driven, hybrid-architecture system designed to centralize release discovery, automate validation, manage configurations, and distribute targeted notifications. It combines the high-concurrency performance of a Go-based polling engine with the reasoning capabilities of LLM-based SRE agents. By leveraging PostgreSQL for both persistent storage and message brokering, the system maintains high reliability and transactional consistency within a streamlined, single-binary deployment.
     6ŌåÆ
     7ŌåÆ## 1. Tech Stack & Infrastructure
     8ŌåÆ
     9ŌåÆ* **Backend / Engine:** Go (Golang). Chosen for its lightweight concurrency model (Goroutines) to handle simultaneous polling across multiple registries.
    10ŌåÆ* **Frontend / Dashboard:** Next.js (React) with Tailwind CSS. Provides a fast, modern control center for viewing release streams and managing configurations.
    11ŌåÆ* **Database, Queue, & Event Bus:** PostgreSQL.
    12ŌåÆ* *Persistent Storage:* Standard relational tables for release metadata and system configurations.
    13ŌåÆ* *Task Queue:* Utilizes `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED` (via the **River** Go library) for robust, concurrent background job processing without race conditions.
    14ŌåÆ* *Pub/Sub:* Utilizes native `LISTEN` and `NOTIFY` for real-time broadcasting to connected clients.
    15ŌåÆ
    16ŌåÆ
    17ŌåÆ* **Intelligence Layer:** LLMs (Gemini / OpenAI-compatible) orchestrated via [ADK-Go](https://google.github.io/adk-go/) v0.5.0 for semantic changelog analysis and autonomous release evaluation.
    18ŌåÆ* **Packaging:** Single binary deployment utilizing Go's `//go:embed` to serve the Next.js static export directly from the Go server.
    19ŌåÆ
    20ŌåÆ## 2. System Design & Abstractions
    21ŌåÆ
    22ŌåÆThe system is decoupled into four primary layers communicating entirely through PostgreSQL using the Transactional Outbox pattern:
    23ŌåÆ
    24ŌåÆ1. **Ingestion Layer** ŌĆö Polling workers and webhook handlers discover new releases from upstream registries.
    25ŌåÆ2. **Notification Routing** ŌĆö River workers send immediate notifications to source-level subscribers on new releases.
    26ŌåÆ3. **Agent Layer** ŌĆö ADK-Go agents research releases, consult context sources, and produce semantic release reports.
    27ŌåÆ4. **Routing & Output** ŌĆö Notification channels (Slack, Discord, email, webhooks) deliver alerts via `Sender`.
    28ŌåÆ
    29ŌåÆA **Project** is the central domain entity ŌĆö it groups multiple ingestion sources, context sources, and notification subscriptions under a single tracked piece of software.
    30ŌåÆ
    31ŌåÆ```mermaid
    32ŌåÆgraph LR
    33ŌåÆ    %% Data Flow
    34ŌåÆ    subgraph Dashboard [API & Dashboard]
    35ŌåÆ        API[REST API] --> |CRUD| PG
    36ŌåÆ        API --> |SSE| Browser[Next.js Dashboard]
    37ŌåÆ    end
    38ŌåÆ
    39ŌåÆ    subgraph Ingestion [Ingestion Layer]
    40ŌåÆ        I_Hub(Docker Hub Poller) --> |IIngestionSource| Engine
    41ŌåÆ        I_Git(GitHub Atom Feed) --> |IIngestionSource| Engine
    42ŌåÆ    end
    43ŌåÆ
    44ŌåÆ    subgraph Core [Event Bus & Queue - PostgreSQL]
    45ŌåÆ        Engine((Go Engine)) --> |INSERT release + jobs\nTransactional Outbox| PG[(PostgreSQL)]
    46ŌåÆ    end
    47ŌåÆ
    48ŌåÆ    subgraph Notification [Notification Routing]
    49ŌåÆ        PG --> |SKIP LOCKED\nRiver notify_release| NotifyWorker[Notify Worker]
    50ŌåÆ        NotifyWorker --> |Sender| O_Slack(Slack)
    51ŌåÆ        NotifyWorker --> |Sender| O_Discord(Discord)
    52ŌåÆ        NotifyWorker --> |Sender| O_Email(Email)
    53ŌåÆ        NotifyWorker --> |Sender| O_Hook(Webhooks)
    54ŌåÆ    end
    55ŌåÆ
    56ŌåÆ    subgraph AgentLayer [Agent Layer]
    57ŌåÆ        PG --> |SKIP LOCKED\nRiver agent_run| AgentWorker[Agent Worker]
    58ŌåÆ        AgentWorker --> |ADK-Go| LLM[LLM Provider]
    59ŌåÆ        AgentWorker --> |Research| ContextSources[Context Sources]
    60ŌåÆ        AgentWorker --> |Produce| SemanticRelease[Semantic Release + Report]
    61ŌåÆ    end
    62ŌåÆ
    63ŌåÆ```
    64ŌåÆ
    65ŌåÆ### Entity Relationship Model
    66ŌåÆ
    67ŌåÆ```mermaid
    68ŌåÆerDiagram
    69ŌåÆ    projects ||--o{ sources : "has many"
    70ŌåÆ    projects ||--o{ context_sources : "has many"
    71ŌåÆ    projects ||--o{ semantic_releases : "has many"
    72ŌåÆ    projects ||--o{ agent_runs : "has many"
    73ŌåÆ    sources ||--o{ releases : "produces"
    74ŌåÆ    subscriptions }o--|| notification_channels : "routes to"
    75ŌåÆ    subscriptions }o--o| sources : "source-level"
    76ŌåÆ    subscriptions }o--o| projects : "project-level"
    77ŌåÆ    semantic_releases ||--o{ semantic_release_sources : "composed of"
    78ŌåÆ    releases ||--o{ semantic_release_sources : "contributes to"
    79ŌåÆ
    80ŌåÆ    projects {
    81ŌåÆ        uuid id PK
    82ŌåÆ        string name UK
    83ŌåÆ        text agent_prompt
    84ŌåÆ        jsonb agent_rules
    85ŌåÆ    }
    86ŌåÆ    sources {
    87ŌåÆ        uuid id PK
    88ŌåÆ        uuid project_id FK
    89ŌåÆ        string provider
    90ŌåÆ        string repository UK
    91ŌåÆ        boolean enabled
    92ŌåÆ    }
    93ŌåÆ    releases {
    94ŌåÆ        uuid id PK
    95ŌåÆ        uuid source_id FK
    96ŌåÆ        string version
    97ŌåÆ        jsonb raw_data
    98ŌåÆ    }
    99ŌåÆ    semantic_releases {
   100ŌåÆ        uuid id PK
   101ŌåÆ        uuid project_id FK
   102ŌåÆ        string version
   103ŌåÆ        jsonb report
   104ŌåÆ        string status
   105ŌåÆ    }
   106ŌåÆ    agent_runs {
   107ŌåÆ        uuid id PK
   108ŌåÆ        uuid project_id FK
   109ŌåÆ        uuid semantic_release_id FK
   110ŌåÆ        string trigger
   111ŌåÆ        string status
   112ŌåÆ    }
   113ŌåÆ    subscriptions {
   114ŌåÆ        uuid id PK
   115ŌåÆ        uuid channel_id FK
   116ŌåÆ        string type
   117ŌåÆ        uuid source_id FK
   118ŌåÆ        uuid project_id FK
   119ŌåÆ    }
   120ŌåÆ    notification_channels {
   121ŌåÆ        uuid id PK
   122ŌåÆ        string type
   123ŌåÆ        string name
   124ŌåÆ        jsonb config
   125ŌåÆ    }
   126ŌåÆ```
   127ŌåÆ
   128ŌåÆ### 2.1 The Event-Driven Backbone (PostgreSQL Only)
   129ŌåÆ
   130ŌåÆComponents do not call each other synchronously. Instead, they rely on PostgreSQL to guarantee delivery:
   131ŌåÆ
   132ŌåÆ* **The Transactional Outbox:** When a new release is detected, the ingestion worker writes the metadata to the `releases` table (linked to its `source_id`) and simultaneously enqueues River jobs (notification and/or agent) *within the exact same SQL transaction*. This guarantees no events are ever lost.
   133ŌåÆ* **Real-time Pub/Sub:** Database triggers use `pg_notify` to broadcast lightweight events (e.g., telling the Next.js UI via SSE to refresh) over standard Postgres connections using `LISTEN`.
   134ŌåÆ* **Reliable Queues:** River workers process jobs using `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`, ensuring exactly-once processing for both notification delivery and agent runs.
   135ŌåÆ* **Project-Centric Model:** All data flows through the `projects` ŌåÆ `sources` ŌåÆ `releases` hierarchy. Subscriptions can attach at the source level (for raw release notifications) or the project level (for semantic release notifications).
   136ŌåÆ
   137ŌåÆ### 2.2 Provider Interfaces (I/O)
   138ŌåÆ
   139ŌåÆAll external integrations are abstracted behind strict Go interfaces.
   140ŌåÆ
   141ŌåÆ* `IIngestionSource`: Standardizes how polling workers fetch data. Adding a new registry (like npm or NuGet) only requires implementing this interface. Each implementation maps to a `source_type` in the database. Current implementations: Docker Hub, GitHub, ECR Public, GitLab, PyPI.
   142ŌåÆ* `Sender`: Standardizes output routing. Each implementation maps to a `type` in the `notification_channels` table (Slack, Discord, email, webhooks).
   143ŌåÆ
   144ŌåÆ### 2.3 REST API & Dashboard
   145ŌåÆ
   146ŌåÆThe Go server exposes a RESTful API (`/api/v1`) serving the Next.js dashboard and external consumers:
   147ŌåÆ
   148ŌåÆ* **Resource CRUD:** Projects, sources, subscriptions, notification channels, context sources ŌĆö all manageable through the API.
   149ŌåÆ* **Read-Only Releases:** Releases and semantic releases are queryable but not directly writable via the API ŌĆö they're created exclusively through the ingestion layer and agent runs.
   150ŌåÆ* **Source Operations:** Manual poll trigger (`POST /sources/{id}/poll`) and channel test notifications (`POST /channels/{id}/test`).

Now I have a comprehensive understanding. Let me provide my findings.

---

## Summary of Codebase Exploration

### 1. Frontend Structure (web/)

**Pages/Routes:**
- `/` ŌĆö Dashboard (home page) ŌĆö main entry point
- `/projects` ŌĆö Projects list view (card & compact modes)
- `/sources` ŌĆö Global sources view across all projects
- `/releases` ŌĆö Releases list
- `/semantic-releases` ŌĆö Semantic releases/analysis view
- `/subscriptions` ŌĆö Notification subscriptions
- `/channels` ŌĆö Notification channels (Slack, Discord, Email, etc.)
- `/todo` ŌĆö Action items/release notification tracking
- `/onboard` ŌĆö Repository dependency scanning & quick onboarding
- `/login` ŌĆö OAuth login page

**Dashboard (Home) - `/app/page.tsx`:**
- Shows **stats cards** (projects, releases, activity metrics)
- **Release trend chart** (time-series visualization)
- **Unified feed** (recent releases across projects)
- **Quick onboard** inline widget (scan repo by URL)
- **Discovery section** (GitHub/Docker Hub search/recommendations)
- Listens to SSE events for real-time updates

### 2. "Source" Data Model (Release Ingestion)

**Source Model** (`/internal/models/source.go`):
A **Source** is what users track ŌĆö a monitored repository/package in an upstream registry (Docker Hub, GitHub, ECR, GitLab, PyPI).

**Key fields:**
- `id`, `project_id`, `provider` (docker, github, ecr, etc.)
- `repository` (e.g., "sentioxyz/changelogue")
- `poll_interval_seconds` ŌĆö how often to check for new releases
- `enabled` ŌĆö active/disabled toggle
- `version_filter_include/exclude` ŌĆö regex patterns to filter versions
- `exclude_prereleases` ŌĆö boolean flag
- `last_polled_at`, `last_error` ŌĆö poll status tracking
- `config` ŌĆö provider-specific JSON config

**How sources are used:**
- Users create **projects** (logical groups)
- Then add **sources** to projects (specific packages/repos to track)
- Sources are polled on an interval to fetch new releases
- Releases are ingested and stored, optionally analyzed by LLM agent
- Both sources and releases can be viewed at project-level or globally

### 3. API Endpoints (Sources/Trackers)

**Sources endpoints** (in `/internal/api/server.go` + `/internal/api/sources.go`):
```
GET    /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/sources       ŌåÆ List sources for project
POST   /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/sources       ŌåÆ Create new source
GET    /api/v1/sources/{id}                       ŌåÆ Get single source
PUT    /api/v1/sources/{id}                       ŌåÆ Update source config
DELETE /api/v1/sources/{id}                       ŌåÆ Delete source
POST   /api/v1/sources/{id}/poll                  ŌåÆ Manually trigger poll
```

**Projects endpoints:**
```
GET    /api/v1/projects                           ŌåÆ List projects
POST   /api/v1/projects                           ŌåÆ Create project
GET    /api/v1/projects/{id}                      ŌåÆ Get project
PUT    /api/v1/projects/{id}                      ŌåÆ Update project
DELETE /api/v1/projects/{id}                      ŌåÆ Delete project
```

**Other key endpoints:**
- Releases: `GET /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/releases` ŌĆö releases for project
- Semantic Releases: LLM-analyzed release reports with urgency/recommendations
- Subscriptions: notifications on new releases
- Channels: Slack/Discord/Email notification destinations
- Context Sources: supplementary data sources for agent analysis (separate from ingestion sources)
- Agent: trigger manual analysis runs or view agent activity

### 4. Dashboard/Home Page

**`/app/page.tsx`** shows:
1. **Discovery section** ŌĆö search GitHub/Docker Hub, add repositories quickly
2. **Quick onboard widget** ŌĆö inline form to scan a repo for dependencies
3. **Stats cards** ŌĆö total projects, active sources, releases this week, pending agent work
4. **Release trend chart** ŌĆö releases over time (7/14/30 days)
5. **Unified feed** ŌĆö recent releases across all projects in chronological order
6. **Empty state** ŌĆö if no projects exist, prompts user to create one or import dependencies

All dashboard data refreshes via SSE (Server-Sent Events) when new releases are detected.

### 5. Key Models & Data Flow

**Project hierarchy:**
```
Project (container)
  Ōö£ŌöĆ Sources (monitored repositories, e.g., "ethers.js" on npm)
  Ōö£ŌöĆ Releases (ingested from sources)
  Ōö£ŌöĆ Semantic Releases (LLM-analyzed reports on releases)
  ŌööŌöĆ Context Sources (supplementary data for agent, e.g., GitHub org repos)
```

**Release flow:**
1. User creates Project ŌåÆ adds Sources (Docker/GitHub repos)
2. Ingestion orchestrator polls sources periodically
3. New releases are fetched and stored
4. If agent is enabled, LLM analyzes release for urgency/changelog/recommendations
5. Notifications sent to subscribed channels
6. Todos created for high-urgency releases

---

**File paths for reference:**
- Frontend pages: `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/web/app/`
- API handlers: `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/api/`
- Models: `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/models/`
- Client types/SDK: `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/web/lib/api/`

Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive understanding. Let me compile my findings:

## Summary of GitHub OAuth & Token Storage Investigation

### **1. GitHub OAuth Login Flow**

**OAuth Scopes Requested:**
- Only **`read:org`** scope is requested (line 39 in `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/auth/oauth.go`)
- This is minimalŌĆöjust enough to read org membership for allowlisting

**User Data Stored on Login:**
In the `users` table (lines 191-199 in `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/db/migrations.go`):
- `id` (UUID, primary key)
- `github_id` (BIGINT, unique)
- `github_login` (VARCHAR)
- `name` (VARCHAR, optional)
- `avatar_url` (TEXT, optional)
- `created_at` / `updated_at` timestamps

**OAuth Callback Flow:**
1. User exchanges code ŌåÆ access token (exchanged but **not stored**)
2. Token fetched temporarily to get user info and orgs
3. Token is discarded after login completes
4. Session cookie created instead (server-side in `sessions` table)

### **2. Token Storage - Current State**

**GitHub OAuth tokens:** NOT stored anywhere
- The access token is used once during callback to fetch user/org info, then discarded (line 66-79 in `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/auth/oauth.go`)
- No `github_token` or similar field in the `users` table

**API Key storage:** Exists but separate from OAuth
- `api_keys` table (lines 124-131 in migrations):
  - `key_hash` (SHA-256 hash, UNIQUE)
  - `key_prefix` (first 12 chars for display)
  - `last_used_at` (for tracking)
- Used for Bearer token authentication, not GitHub OAuth

**Token usage in codebase:**
- `internal/onboard/scanner.go` (lines 47-55) accepts a `GITHUB_TOKEN` environment variable for repo scanning
- The scanner uses it for GitHub API calls, but this is **environment-level**, not per-user

### **3. User Model & Session Structure**

**User struct** (`/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/auth/user.go`):
```go
type User struct {
    ID          string `json:"id"`          // UUID
    GitHubID    int64  `json:"github_id"`
    GitHubLogin string `json:"github_login"`
    Name        string `json:"name,omitempty"`
    AvatarURL   string `json:"avatar_url,omitempty"`
}
```

**Session storage:**
- `sessions` table: `(id, user_id, expires_at, created_at)` ŌĆö server-side, HttpOnly cookie
- HMAC-signed cookie: `"sessionID.hmacHex"` for tampering protection
- 7-day expiration

### **Key Insights**

1. **No stored GitHub tokens** ŌĆö Current design uses OAuth only for authentication, not authorization to GitHub APIs
2. **Minimal OAuth scope** ŌĆö Only `read:org` (GitHub limits exposure)
3. **Separation of concerns** ŌĆö API keys are independent from user auth; GitHub token access is environment-level via `GITHUB_TOKEN`
4. **Allowlisting via orgs** ŌĆö Can restrict users by GitHub org membership fetched during login
5. **Session pattern is robust** ŌĆö HMAC-signed, server-side, with cleanup

This architecture intentionally **does not bind GitHub tokens to individual users**ŌĆöinstead relying on environment-level access for backend operations and user-level auth via sessions.

Excellent! I have a comprehensive understanding now. Let me create a final summary of my findings: 

## Summary: Ingestion Architecture & Source Configuration

### 1. **Ingestion Providers (6 Supported)**

The system implements 6 upstream registry providers via the `IIngestionSource` interface:

| Provider | File | API Endpoint | Key Details |
|----------|------|--------------|------------|
| **Docker Hub** | `dockerhub.go` | `/v2/repositories/{Repository}/tags/` | Fetches latest 25 tags ordered by `last_updated` |
| **GitHub** | `github.go` | `/repos/{Repository}/releases` | Fetches 30 releases; supports auth token via `GITHUB_TOKEN` env var; captures changelog + prerelease flag |
| **PyPI** | `pypi.go` | `/pypi/{Package}/json` | Uses PyPI JSON API; handles yanked versions; stores release URL in metadata |
| **npm** | `npm.go` | `/{Package}` | Registry API returns all versions with publish timestamps; filters metadata keys (`created`, `modified`) |
| **ECR Public** | `ecr_public.go` | `POST /describeImageTags` (Gallery API) | Pagination support; requires `registry_alias/repo_name` format; captures `imagePushedAt` timestamps |
| **GitLab** | `gitlab.go` | `/api/v4/projects/{ProjectID}/releases` | Supports GitLab token via `GITLAB_TOKEN` env var; URL-encodes project paths; captures prerelease flag |

### 2. **Source Data Model & Configuration**

**Database Table: `sources`** (defined in `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/db/migrations.go`, lines 24ŌĆō38)

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS sources (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY,
    project_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES projects(id),
    provider VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,              -- "dockerhub", "github", etc.
    repository VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,           -- package/repo name (e.g., "golang" for Docker, "user/repo" for GitHub)
    poll_interval_seconds INT DEFAULT 86400,    -- 24 hours default
    enabled BOOLEAN DEFAULT true,
    config JSONB,                               -- flexible per-provider config
    last_polled_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    last_error TEXT,
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    UNIQUE(provider, repository)                -- no duplicate (provider, repo) pairs
);
```

**Recent migrations added filtering:**
- `version_filter_include` (TEXT) ŌĆö include pattern
- `version_filter_exclude` (TEXT) ŌĆö exclude pattern  
- `exclude_prereleases` (BOOLEAN DEFAULT false) ŌĆö skip prerelease versions

### 3. **How Sources are Added & Configured**

**Via REST API** (`/api/v1/projects/{projectId}/sources`):
- `POST` creates a new source with provider, repository, poll interval
- `PUT` updates poll interval, enable/disable, filters
- Sources permanently linked to a project (foreign key on `project_id`)

**Factory Function** (`BuildSource` in `loader.go`, lines 87ŌĆō105):
```go
switch sourceType {
case "dockerhub":   return NewDockerHubSource(...)
case "github":      return NewGitHubSource(...)
case "ecr-public":  return NewECRPublicSource(...)
case "gitlab":      return NewGitLabSource(...)
case "pypi":        return NewPyPISource(...)
case "npm":         return NewNpmSource(...)
}
```

### 4. **Polling & Crawling Architecture**

**Orchestrator Flow** (`orchestrator.go`):

1. **Service Startup**: `NewOrchestrator()` creates polling engine with configurable interval (typically every N seconds)
2. **Dynamic Loader**: On each poll cycle, `SourceLoader.LoadEnabledSources()` queries the DB for all enabled sources with their metadata
3. **Schedule Check**: For each source, checks if `now - last_polled_at >= poll_interval_seconds`
4. **Fetch & Persist**: For each due source:
   - Calls `source.FetchNewReleases(ctx)` ŌåÆ HTTP request to upstream API
   - Returns `[]IngestionResult` (version, changelog, metadata, timestamp)
   - Passes results to `Service.ProcessResults()` which calls `store.IngestRelease()` for each

**Transactional Outbox** (`pgstore.go`):
- Each release insertion is wrapped in a transaction with River job enqueueing
- Atomically inserts to `releases` table + enqueues `NotifyJobArgs` to River queue
- Duplicate versions (unique key violation) are skipped with a debug log

**Result Data Model** (`IngestionResult` in `source.go`):
```go
type IngestionResult struct {
    Repository string            // e.g., "golang" (normalization from provider)
    RawVersion string            // e.g., "v1.25.0" (raw tag/version string)
    Changelog  string            // release notes (GitHub, GitLab only)
    Metadata   map[string]string // flexible: release_url, prerelease, etc.
    Timestamp  time.Time         // when the version was released upstream
}
```

### 5. **Database Schema Cascade**

```
projects
  Ōö£ŌöĆŌåÆ sources (one provider per source row)
  Ōöé     ŌööŌöĆŌåÆ releases (all versions discovered by this source)
  Ōöé           ŌööŌöĆŌåÆ semantic_release_sources (join table)
  Ōöé                 ŌööŌöĆŌåÆ semantic_releases (AI-correlated releases)
  ŌööŌöĆŌåÆ subscriptions (notification routing)
        ŌööŌöĆŌåÆ notification_channels (Slack, Discord, email, webhooks)
```

**Key Constraints:**
- `releases` has UNIQUE(source_id, version) ŌĆö prevents duplicate version ingestion
- Release notification is automatic via River's transactional outbox pattern
- Polling sources are never manually written through API; created exclusively via REST source registration

### 6. **Error Handling & Resilience**

- HTTP errors mapped to human-readable messages (`httpStatusError` in `source.go`)
- Rate limiting (HTTP 429) gracefully handled with retry logic in River
- Failed polls update `sources.last_error` field for debugging
- Duplicate releases silently skipped (debug log), not fatal
- Context timeout propagated through all HTTP calls

---

This architecture ensures scalability (horizontal polling), transactional consistency (no event loss), and operator visibility (poll status per source) within a single binary deployment.

Excellent! I have a comprehensive understanding now. Let me create a final summary of my findings: 

## Summary: Ingestion Architecture & Source Configuration

### 1. **Ingestion Providers (6 Supported)**

|----------|------|--------------|------------|
| **Docker Hub** | `dockerhub.go` | `/v2/repositories/{Repository}/tags/` | Fetches latest 25 tags ordered by `last_updated` |
| **GitHub** | `github.go` | `/repos/{Repository}/releases` | Fetches 30 releases; supports auth token via `GITHUB_TOKEN` env var; captures changelog + prerelease flag |
| **PyPI** | `pypi.go` | `/pypi/{Package}/json` | Uses PyPI JSON API; handles yanked versions; stores release URL in metadata |
| **npm** | `npm.go` | `/{Package}` | Registry API returns all versions with publish timestamps; filters metadata keys (`created`, `modified`) |
| **ECR Public** | `ecr_public.go` | `POST /describeImageTags` (Gallery API) | Pagination support; requires `registry_alias/repo_name` format; captures `imagePushedAt` timestamps |
| **GitLab** | `gitlab.go` | `/api/v4/projects/{ProjectID}/releases` | Supports GitLab token via `GITLAB_TOKEN` env var; URL-encodes project paths; captures prerelease flag |

    11ŌåÆ)
    12ŌåÆ
    13ŌåÆ// Dependencies holds all external dependencies required for registering API routes.
    14ŌåÆtype Dependencies struct {
    15ŌåÆ	DB                    *pgxpool.Pool
    16ŌåÆ	ProjectsStore         ProjectsStore
    17ŌåÆ	ReleasesStore         ReleasesStore
    18ŌåÆ	SubscriptionsStore    SubscriptionsStore
    19ŌåÆ	SourcesStore          SourcesStore
    20ŌåÆ	ChannelsStore         ChannelsStore
    21ŌåÆ	ContextSourcesStore   ContextSourcesStore
    22ŌåÆ	SemanticReleasesStore SemanticReleasesStore
    23ŌåÆ	AgentStore            AgentStore
    24ŌåÆ	TodosStore            TodosStore
    25ŌåÆ	OnboardStore          OnboardStore
    26ŌåÆ	PublicURL             string
    27ŌåÆ	KeyStore              KeyStore
    28ŌåÆ	SessionValidator      SessionValidator
    29ŌåÆ	HealthChecker         HealthChecker

    35ŌåÆ
    36ŌåÆ// RegisterRoutes registers all API v1 routes on the given ServeMux.
    37ŌåÆfunc RegisterRoutes(mux *http.ServeMux, deps Dependencies) {
    38ŌåÆ	// Public chain: no auth required.
    39ŌåÆ	publicChain := Chain(RequestID, Logger, Recovery)
    40ŌåÆ
    41ŌåÆ	// Authenticated chain: includes auth unless NoAuth is set.
    42ŌåÆ	chain := publicChain
    43ŌåÆ	if !deps.NoAuth {
    44ŌåÆ		chain = Chain(RequestID, Logger, Recovery, RateLimit(10, 20), Auth(deps.KeyStore, deps.SessionValidator))
    45ŌåÆ	}
    46ŌåÆ
    47ŌåÆ	// Projects (CRUD)
    48ŌåÆ	projects := NewProjectsHandler(deps.ProjectsStore)
    49ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/projects", chain(http.HandlerFunc(projects.List)))
    50ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("POST /api/v1/projects", chain(http.HandlerFunc(projects.Create)))
    51ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/projects/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(projects.Get)))
    52ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("PUT /api/v1/projects/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(projects.Update)))
    53ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("DELETE /api/v1/projects/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(projects.Delete)))
    54ŌåÆ
    55ŌåÆ	// Sources (nested under projects)
    56ŌåÆ	sources := NewSourcesHandler(deps.SourcesStore, deps.IngestionService, deps.HTTPClient)
    57ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/sources", chain(http.HandlerFunc(sources.List)))
    58ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("POST /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/sources", chain(http.HandlerFunc(sources.Create)))
    59ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/sources/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(sources.Get)))
    60ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("PUT /api/v1/sources/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(sources.Update)))
    61ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("DELETE /api/v1/sources/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(sources.Delete)))
    62ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("POST /api/v1/sources/{id}/poll", chain(http.HandlerFunc(sources.FetchReleases)))
    63ŌåÆ
    64ŌåÆ	// Context Sources (nested under projects)
    65ŌåÆ	contextSources := NewContextSourcesHandler(deps.ContextSourcesStore)
    66ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/context-sources", chain(http.HandlerFunc(contextSources.List)))
    67ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("POST /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/context-sources", chain(http.HandlerFunc(contextSources.Create)))
    68ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/context-sources/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(contextSources.Get)))
    69ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("PUT /api/v1/context-sources/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(contextSources.Update)))
    70ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("DELETE /api/v1/context-sources/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(contextSources.Delete)))
    71ŌåÆ
    72ŌåÆ	// Releases (read-only, nested under sources and projects)
    73ŌåÆ	releases := NewReleasesHandler(deps.ReleasesStore)
    74ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/releases", chain(http.HandlerFunc(releases.List)))
    75ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/sources/{id}/releases", chain(http.HandlerFunc(releases.ListBySource)))
    76ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/releases", chain(http.HandlerFunc(releases.ListByProject)))
    77ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/releases/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(releases.Get)))
    78ŌåÆ
    79ŌåÆ	// Semantic Releases (nested under projects + top-level list)
    80ŌåÆ	semanticReleases := NewSemanticReleasesHandler(deps.SemanticReleasesStore)
    81ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/semantic-releases", chain(http.HandlerFunc(semanticReleases.ListAll)))
    82ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/semantic-releases", chain(http.HandlerFunc(semanticReleases.List)))
    83ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/semantic-releases/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(semanticReleases.Get)))
    84ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/semantic-releases/{id}/sources", chain(http.HandlerFunc(semanticReleases.ListSources)))
    85ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("DELETE /api/v1/semantic-releases/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(semanticReleases.Delete)))
    86ŌåÆ
    87ŌåÆ	// Subscriptions (CRUD)
    88ŌåÆ	subscriptions := NewSubscriptionsHandler(deps.SubscriptionsStore)
    89ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/subscriptions", chain(http.HandlerFunc(subscriptions.List)))
    90ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("POST /api/v1/subscriptions", chain(http.HandlerFunc(subscriptions.Create)))
    91ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("POST /api/v1/subscriptions/batch", chain(http.HandlerFunc(subscriptions.BatchCreate)))
    92ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("DELETE /api/v1/subscriptions/batch", chain(http.HandlerFunc(subscriptions.BatchDelete)))
    93ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/subscriptions/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(subscriptions.Get)))
    94ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("PUT /api/v1/subscriptions/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(subscriptions.Update)))
    95ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("DELETE /api/v1/subscriptions/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(subscriptions.Delete)))
    96ŌåÆ
    97ŌåÆ	// Channels (CRUD + test)
    98ŌåÆ	channels := NewChannelsHandler(deps.ChannelsStore, routing.NewSenders(), deps.PublicURL)
    99ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/channels", chain(http.HandlerFunc(channels.List)))
   100ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("POST /api/v1/channels", chain(http.HandlerFunc(channels.Create)))
   101ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/channels/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(channels.Get)))
   102ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("PUT /api/v1/channels/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(channels.Update)))
   103ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("DELETE /api/v1/channels/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(channels.Delete)))
   104ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("POST /api/v1/channels/{id}/test", chain(http.HandlerFunc(channels.Test)))
   105ŌåÆ
   106ŌåÆ	// Agent
   107ŌåÆ	agent := NewAgentHandler(deps.AgentStore)
   108ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("POST /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/agent/run", chain(http.HandlerFunc(agent.TriggerRun)))
   109ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/agent/runs", chain(http.HandlerFunc(agent.ListRuns)))
   110ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/agent-runs/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(agent.GetRun)))
   111ŌåÆ
   112ŌåÆ	// Todos
   113ŌåÆ	todos := NewTodosHandler(deps.TodosStore, deps.PublicURL)
   114ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/todos", chain(http.HandlerFunc(todos.List)))
   115ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/todos/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(todos.Get)))
   116ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("PATCH /api/v1/todos/{id}/acknowledge", chain(http.HandlerFunc(todos.Acknowledge)))
   117ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("PATCH /api/v1/todos/{id}/resolve", chain(http.HandlerFunc(todos.Resolve)))
   118ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("PATCH /api/v1/todos/{id}/reopen", chain(http.HandlerFunc(todos.Reopen)))
   119ŌåÆ	// One-click endpoints for notification links (GET so they work as <a href>)
   120ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/todos/{id}/acknowledge", chain(http.HandlerFunc(todos.Acknowledge)))
   121ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/todos/{id}/resolve", chain(http.HandlerFunc(todos.Resolve)))
   122ŌåÆ
   123ŌåÆ	// Onboard (repo dependency scanning)
   124ŌåÆ	ob := NewOnboardHandler(deps.OnboardStore)
   125ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("POST /api/v1/onboard/scan", chain(http.HandlerFunc(ob.Scan)))
   126ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/onboard/scans/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(ob.GetScan)))
   127ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("POST /api/v1/onboard/scans/{id}/apply", chain(http.HandlerFunc(ob.Apply)))
   128ŌåÆ

   130ŌåÆ	providers := NewProvidersHandler()

   132ŌåÆ
   133ŌåÆ	// Discovery (public ŌĆö no auth, proxies external APIs)
   134ŌåÆ	discover := NewDiscoverHandler(deps.HTTPClient, "", "")
   135ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/discover/github", publicChain(http.HandlerFunc(discover.GitHub)))
   136ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/discover/dockerhub", publicChain(http.HandlerFunc(discover.DockerHub)))
   137ŌåÆ
   138ŌåÆ	// SSE events
   139ŌåÆ	events := NewEventsHandler(deps.Broadcaster)

   142ŌåÆ	// Health (public ŌĆö no auth middleware)
   143ŌåÆ	health := NewHealthHandler(deps.HealthChecker)

   147ŌåÆ}

1ŌåÆpackage onboard
     2ŌåÆ
     3ŌåÆimport (
     4ŌåÆ	"context"
     5ŌåÆ	"encoding/json"
     6ŌåÆ	"fmt"
     7ŌåÆ	"log/slog"
     8ŌåÆ	"time"
     9ŌåÆ
    10ŌåÆ	"github.com/jackc/pgx/v5/pgxpool"
    11ŌåÆ	"github.com/riverqueue/river"
    12ŌåÆ	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/models"
    13ŌåÆ	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/queue"
    14ŌåÆ)
    15ŌåÆ
    16ŌåÆ// ScanStore is the data access interface for the scan worker.
    17ŌåÆtype ScanStore interface {
    18ŌåÆ	GetOnboardScan(ctx context.Context, id string) (*models.OnboardScan, error)
    19ŌåÆ	UpdateOnboardScanStatus(ctx context.Context, id, status string, results json.RawMessage, scanErr string) error
    20ŌåÆ}
    21ŌåÆ
    22ŌåÆ// ScanWorker processes scan_dependencies River jobs.
    23ŌåÆtype ScanWorker struct {
    24ŌåÆ	river.WorkerDefaults[queue.ScanDependenciesJobArgs]
    25ŌåÆ	store     ScanStore
    26ŌåÆ	scanner   *Scanner
    27ŌåÆ	extractor *DependencyExtractor
    28ŌåÆ	pool      *pgxpool.Pool
    29ŌåÆ}
    30ŌåÆ
    31ŌåÆ// NewScanWorker creates a ScanWorker. extractor may be nil if GOOGLE_API_KEY is not set.
    32ŌåÆfunc NewScanWorker(store ScanStore, scanner *Scanner, extractor *DependencyExtractor, pool *pgxpool.Pool) *ScanWorker {
    33ŌåÆ	return &ScanWorker{
    34ŌåÆ		store:     store,
    35ŌåÆ		scanner:   scanner,
    36ŌåÆ		extractor: extractor,
    37ŌåÆ		pool:      pool,
    38ŌåÆ	}
    39ŌåÆ}
    40ŌåÆ
    41ŌåÆfunc (w *ScanWorker) Timeout(_ *river.Job[queue.ScanDependenciesJobArgs]) time.Duration {
    42ŌåÆ	return 3 * time.Minute
    43ŌåÆ}
    44ŌåÆ
    45ŌåÆfunc (w *ScanWorker) Work(ctx context.Context, job *river.Job[queue.ScanDependenciesJobArgs]) error {
    46ŌåÆ	scanID := job.Args.ScanID
    47ŌåÆ	slog.Info("scan worker picked up job", "scan_id", scanID, "attempt", job.Attempt)
    48ŌåÆ
    49ŌåÆ	// Load scan
    50ŌåÆ	scan, err := w.store.GetOnboardScan(ctx, scanID)
    51ŌåÆ	if err != nil {
    52ŌåÆ		return fmt.Errorf("load scan %s: %w", scanID, err)
    53ŌåÆ	}
    54ŌåÆ
    55ŌåÆ	// Mark processing
    56ŌåÆ	if err := w.store.UpdateOnboardScanStatus(ctx, scanID, "processing", nil, ""); err != nil {
    57ŌåÆ		return fmt.Errorf("update status to processing: %w", err)
    58ŌåÆ	}
    59ŌåÆ
    60ŌåÆ	// Parse repo URL
    61ŌåÆ	owner, repo, err := ParseRepoURL(scan.RepoURL)
    62ŌåÆ	if err != nil {
    63ŌåÆ		w.store.UpdateOnboardScanStatus(ctx, scanID, "failed", nil, err.Error())
    64ŌåÆ		return fmt.Errorf("parse repo URL: %w", err)
    65ŌåÆ	}
    66ŌåÆ
    67ŌåÆ	// Fetch dependency files
    68ŌåÆ	files, err := w.scanner.FetchDependencyFiles(ctx, owner, repo)
    69ŌåÆ	if err != nil {
    70ŌåÆ		w.store.UpdateOnboardScanStatus(ctx, scanID, "failed", nil, err.Error())
    71ŌåÆ		return fmt.Errorf("fetch dependency files: %w", err)
    72ŌåÆ	}
    73ŌåÆ
    74ŌåÆ	slog.Info("found dependency files", "scan_id", scanID, "count", len(files))
    75ŌåÆ
    76ŌåÆ	if len(files) == 0 {
    77ŌåÆ		// No dependency files ŌĆö store empty results
    78ŌåÆ		emptyResults, _ := json.Marshal([]models.ScannedDependency{})
    79ŌåÆ		w.store.UpdateOnboardScanStatus(ctx, scanID, "completed", emptyResults, "")
    80ŌåÆ		w.notifyScanComplete(ctx, scanID)
    81ŌåÆ		return nil
    82ŌåÆ	}
    83ŌåÆ
    84ŌåÆ	// Extract dependencies via LLM
    85ŌåÆ	if w.extractor == nil {
    86ŌåÆ		w.store.UpdateOnboardScanStatus(ctx, scanID, "failed", nil, "LLM not configured (set GOOGLE_API_KEY or OPENAI_API_KEY)")
    87ŌåÆ		return river.JobCancel(fmt.Errorf("extractor not configured"))
    88ŌåÆ	}
    89ŌåÆ
    90ŌåÆ	deps, err := w.extractor.Extract(ctx, files)
    91ŌåÆ	if err != nil {
    92ŌåÆ		w.store.UpdateOnboardScanStatus(ctx, scanID, "failed", nil, err.Error())
    93ŌåÆ		return fmt.Errorf("extract dependencies: %w", err)
    94ŌåÆ	}
    95ŌåÆ
    96ŌåÆ	slog.Info("extracted dependencies", "scan_id", scanID, "count", len(deps))
    97ŌåÆ
    98ŌåÆ	results, err := json.Marshal(deps)
    99ŌåÆ	if err != nil {
   100ŌåÆ		return fmt.Errorf("marshal results: %w", err)
   101ŌåÆ	}
   102ŌåÆ
   103ŌåÆ	if err := w.store.UpdateOnboardScanStatus(ctx, scanID, "completed", results, ""); err != nil {
   104ŌåÆ		return fmt.Errorf("update scan completed: %w", err)
   105ŌåÆ	}
   106ŌåÆ
   107ŌåÆ	w.notifyScanComplete(ctx, scanID)
   108ŌåÆ	return nil
   109ŌåÆ}
   110ŌåÆ
   111ŌåÆ// notifyScanComplete sends a pg_notify on the release_events channel.
   112ŌåÆfunc (w *ScanWorker) notifyScanComplete(ctx context.Context, scanID string) {
   113ŌåÆ	payload := fmt.Sprintf(`{"type":"scan_complete","id":"%s"}`, scanID)
   114ŌåÆ	_, err := w.pool.Exec(ctx, "SELECT pg_notify('release_events', $1)", payload)
   115ŌåÆ	if err != nil {
   116ŌåÆ		slog.Error("pg_notify failed", "scan_id", scanID, "err", err)
   117ŌåÆ	}
   118ŌåÆ}
   119ŌåÆ

Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive understanding. Let me create a summary report: 

## Summary: Existing Discovery, Suggestion, and Onboarding Features

I've thoroughly analyzed the Changelogue codebase. Here's what already exists for discovering and suggesting repositories/dependencies:

---

### **1. Discovery Section (Frontend)**

**Location:** `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/web/components/dashboard/discovery-section.tsx`

This is a **marquee carousel** on the dashboard home page that shows trending repositories:
- Displays trending GitHub and Docker Hub repositories in a horizontal scrolling ticker
- Shows repo name, star count, and provider icon
- **One-click tracking**: Users can click a "Star + Plus" button to immediately track a repo
  - Creates a new project
  - Adds a source with daily polling interval (86400 seconds)
  - Auto-polls the source once
  - Refreshes the projects list

**Features:**
- Hover to pause scrolling
- Already-tracked repos show a checkmark and are visually de-emphasized
- Caches trending data for 1 hour to reduce API calls
- Shows loading skeleton while fetching

---

### **2. Discovery API (Backend)**

**Location:** `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/api/discover.go`

Two endpoints exposed via `/discover/` routes (no auth required):

#### `GET /api/v1/discover/github`
- **Query params:**
  - `q` ŌĆö custom search query (optional)
  - `language` ŌĆö filter by programming language (optional)
- **Default behavior:** Returns trending GitHub repos from the last 7 days with 10+ stars, sorted by star count
- Returns up to 25 results
- Caches results for 1 hour
- Example response fields: `name`, `full_name`, `description`, `stars`, `language`, `url`, `avatar_url`, `provider`

#### `GET /api/v1/discover/dockerhub`
- **Query params:** `q` ŌĆö search term (defaults to "nginx")
- Returns up to 25 Docker Hub repositories
- Caches results for 1 hour
- Response fields: `name`, `full_name`, `description`, `stars`, `url`, `provider`

---

### **3. Quick Onboard (Dependency Scanner)**

**Frontend:** `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/web/app/onboard/page.tsx`

A dedicated onboarding wizard with 4 steps:

**Step 1: Input**
- User enters GitHub repo URL (accepts `owner/repo` or full HTTPS URL)
- Can be triggered from dashboard quick-onboard bar or via query string `?repo=`

**Step 2: Scanning**
- Shows progress indicators (Queued ŌåÆ Fetching files ŌåÆ Extracting deps)
- Elapsed time counter
- Uses River job queue (async background processing)

**Step 3: Results**
- Table showing detected dependencies with:
  - Checkboxes to select which to track (all selected by default)
  - Dependency name, version, ecosystem badge, upstream repo
  - Dropdown to assign each dependency to existing project or create new one
  - Ecosystem-specific color badges (Go, NPM, PyPI, Cargo, etc.)
- "Track Selected" button to apply selections

**Step 4: Applied**
- Shows success confirmation with count of created sources/projects
- Lists any skipped dependencies
- Links to view projects

**Supported Dependency Files:**
- `go.mod`, `package.json`, `requirements.txt`, `Pipfile`, `pyproject.toml`, `Cargo.toml`, `Gemfile`, `pom.xml`, `build.gradle`, `build.gradle.kts`, `Dockerfile`, `docker-compose.yml`, `docker-compose.yaml`

---

### **4. Onboarding Workflow (Backend)**

**Location:**
- `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/onboard/scanner.go` ŌĆö Fetches dependency files from GitHub
- `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/onboard/extractor.go` ŌĆö LLM-powered dependency extraction (Gemini or OpenAI)
- `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/onboard/worker.go` ŌĆö River job worker
- `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/api/onboard.go` ŌĆö HTTP handlers

**Process:**
1. **POST `/api/v1/onboard/scan`** ŌåÆ Creates an `OnboardScan` record with status "pending"
   - Prevents concurrent scans for same repo (returns 409 if active scan exists)
   - Enqueues a `ScanDependenciesJobArgs` River job
2. **Worker picks up job** ŌåÆ Transitions to "processing"
   - Parses GitHub repo URL
   - Fetches all dependency files via GitHub API (recursive tree view)
   - Extracts dependencies using LLM prompt (Gemini 2.0 Flash by default, OpenAI 4o-mini as fallback)
   - Stores results as JSON array of `ScannedDependency` objects
   - Updates scan status to "completed" or "failed"
   - Sends PG NOTIFY on `release_events` channel
3. **GET `/api/v1/onboard/scans/{id}`** ŌåÆ Polls scan status (frontend polls every 2 seconds)
4. **POST `/api/v1/onboard/scans/{id}/apply`** ŌåÆ User applies selections
   - Takes array of `OnboardSelection` objects
   - For each selection: creates Project + Source (or adds to existing project)
   - Returns counts of created resources and any skipped entries

**LLM Extraction:**
- Uses system prompt to enforce JSON array output (no markdown, no explanations)
- Extracts: name, version, ecosystem, upstream_repo (GitHub best-guess), provider (github/dockerhub/gitlab/ecr_public)
- Supports both Gemini and OpenAI APIs
- Handles markdown-fenced and raw JSON responses

---

### **5. Dashboard Integration**

**Location:** `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/web/app/page.tsx`

Three discovery/onboarding entry points:
1. **DiscoverySection component** ŌĆö Trending marquee at top (see #1)
2. **Quick Onboard inline bar** ŌĆö Text input + "Scan" button
   - Routes to `/onboard?repo={url}` on Enter or button click
   - Auto-starts scan from query param
3. **DashboardEmptyState** ŌĆö When no projects exist, shows "Create a Project" button linking to `/projects`

---

### **6. Frontend Client API**

**Location:** `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/web/lib/api/client.ts`

Exposed client methods:
```typescript
discover.github(params?: { q?: string; language?: string })
discover.dockerhub(params?: { q?: string })
onboard.scan(repoUrl: string)
onboard.getScan(id: string)
onboard.apply(id: string, selections: OnboardSelection[])
```

---

### **Key Types**

```typescript
// Discovery
DiscoverItem: { name, full_name, description, stars, language?, url, avatar_url?, provider }

// Onboarding
OnboardScan: { id, repo_url, status, results?, error?, created_at, started_at?, completed_at? }
ScannedDependency: { name, version, ecosystem, upstream_repo, provider }
OnboardSelection: { dep_name, upstream_repo, provider, project_id?, new_project_name? }
```

---

### **What This Means for Your Feature**

Ō£ģ **Already implemented:**
- Full repo dependency scanning (GitHub-only)
- Trending discovery carousel (GitHub + Docker Hub)
- Bulk project/source creation from scan results
- LLM-powered dependency extraction

ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ **Potential overlaps to avoid:**
- Don't duplicate the Quick Onboard page
- Don't re-implement the trending carousel (reuse DiscoverySection)
- Don't add another repo URL input (use existing onboard flow or discovery)

­¤Ä» **Extension points:**
- Add more discovery sources (beyond GitHub/Docker Hub trending)
- Enhance filtering/search in discovery
- Add personalized recommendations (based on existing projects)
- Extend onboarding to support non-GitHub sources

1ŌåÆ# Visual Companion Guide 2ŌåÆ 3ŌåÆBrowser-based visual brainstorming companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and options. 4ŌåÆ 5ŌåÆ## When to Use 6ŌåÆ 7ŌåÆDecide per-question, not per-session. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?** 8ŌåÆ 9ŌåÆ**Use the browser** when the content itself is visual: 10ŌåÆ 11ŌåÆ- **UI mockups** ŌĆö wireframes, layouts, navigation structures, component designs 12ŌåÆ- **Architecture diagrams** ŌĆö system components, data flow, relationship maps 13ŌåÆ- **Side-by-side visual comparisons** ŌĆö comparing two layouts, two color schemes, two design directions 14ŌåÆ- **Design polish** ŌĆö when the question is about look and feel, spacing, visual hierarchy 15ŌåÆ- **Spatial relationships** ŌĆö state machines, flowcharts, entity relationships rendered as diagrams 16ŌåÆ 17ŌåÆ**Use the terminal** when the content is text or tabular: 18ŌåÆ 19ŌåÆ- **Requirements and scope questions** ŌĆö "what does X mean?", "which features are in scope?" 20ŌåÆ- **Conceptual A/B/C choices** ŌĆö picking between approaches described in words 21ŌåÆ- **Tradeoff lists** ŌĆö pros/cons, comparison tables 22ŌåÆ- **Technical decisions** ŌĆö API design, data modeling, architectural approach selection 23ŌåÆ- **Clarifying questions** ŌĆö anything where the answer is words, not a visual preference 24ŌåÆ 25ŌåÆA question *about* a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What kind of wizard do you want?" is conceptual ŌĆö use the terminal. "Which of these wizard layouts feels right?" is visual ŌĆö use the browser. 26ŌåÆ 27ŌåÆ## How It Works 28ŌåÆ 29ŌåÆThe server watches a directory for HTML files and serves the newest one to the browser. You write HTML content, the user sees it in their browser and can click to select options. Selections are recorded to a `.events` file that you read on your next turn. 30ŌåÆ 31ŌåÆ**Content fragments vs full documents:** If your HTML file starts with `/.superpowers/brainstorm/` for the session directory. 46ŌåÆ 47ŌåÆ**Note:** Pass the project root as `--project-dir` so mockups persist in `.superpowers/brainstorm/` and survive server restarts. Without it, files go to `/tmp` and get cleaned up. Remind the user to add `.superpowers/` to `.gitignore` if it's not already there. 48ŌåÆ 49ŌåÆ**Launching the server by platform:** 50ŌåÆ 51ŌåÆ**Claude Code (macOS / Linux):** 52ŌåÆ```bash 53ŌåÆ# Default mode works ŌĆö the script backgrounds the server itself 54ŌåÆscripts/start-server.sh --project-dir /path/to/project 55ŌåÆ``` 56ŌåÆ 57ŌåÆ**Claude Code (Windows):** 58ŌåÆ```bash 59ŌåÆ# Windows auto-detects and uses foreground mode, which blocks the tool call. 60ŌåÆ# Use run_in_background: true on the Bash tool call so the server survives 61ŌåÆ# across conversation turns. 62ŌåÆscripts/start-server.sh --project-dir /path/to/project 63ŌåÆ``` 64ŌåÆWhen calling this via the Bash tool, set `run_in_background: true`. Then read `$SCREEN_DIR/.server-info` on the next turn to get the URL and port. 65ŌåÆ 66ŌåÆ**Codex:** 67ŌåÆ```bash 68ŌåÆ# Codex reaps background processes. The script auto-detects CODEX_CI and 69ŌåÆ# switches to foreground mode. Run it normally ŌĆö no extra flags needed. 70ŌåÆscripts/start-server.sh --project-dir /path/to/project 71ŌåÆ``` 72ŌåÆ 73ŌåÆ**Gemini CLI:** 74ŌåÆ```bash 75ŌåÆ# Use --foreground and set is_background: true on your shell tool call 76ŌåÆ# so the process survives across turns 77ŌåÆscripts/start-server.sh --project-dir /path/to/project --foreground 78ŌåÆ``` 79ŌåÆ 80ŌåÆ**Other environments:** The server must keep running in the background across conversation turns. If your environment reaps detached processes, use `--foreground` and launch the command with your platform's background execution mechanism. 81ŌåÆ 82ŌåÆIf the URL is unreachable from your browser (common in remote/containerized setups), bind a non-loopback host: 83ŌåÆ 84ŌåÆ```bash 85ŌåÆscripts/start-server.sh \ 86ŌåÆ --project-dir /path/to/project \ 87ŌåÆ --host 0.0.0.0 \ 88ŌåÆ --url-host localhost 89ŌåÆ``` 90ŌåÆ 91ŌåÆUse `--url-host` to control what hostname is printed in the returned URL JSON. 92ŌåÆ 93ŌåÆ## The Loop 94ŌåÆ 95ŌåÆ1. **Check server is alive**, then **write HTML** to a new file in `screen_dir`: 96ŌåÆ - Before each write, check that `$SCREEN_DIR/.server-info` exists. If it doesn't (or `.server-stopped` exists), the server has shut down ŌĆö restart it with `start-server.sh` before continuing. The server auto-exits after 30 minutes of inactivity. 97ŌåÆ - Use semantic filenames: `platform.html`, `visual-style.html`, `layout.html` 98ŌåÆ - **Never reuse filenames** ŌĆö each screen gets a fresh file 99ŌåÆ - Use Write tool ŌĆö **never use cat/heredoc** (dumps noise into terminal) 100ŌåÆ - Server automatically serves the newest file 101ŌåÆ 102ŌåÆ2. **Tell user what to expect and end your turn:** 103ŌåÆ - Remind them of the URL (every step, not just first) 104ŌåÆ - Give a brief text summary of what's on screen (e.g., "Showing 3 layout options for the homepage") 105ŌåÆ - Ask them to respond in the terminal: "Take a look and let me know what you think. Click to select an option if you'd like." 106ŌåÆ 107ŌåÆ3. **On your next turn** ŌĆö after the user responds in the terminal: 108ŌåÆ - Read `$SCREEN_DIR/.events` if it exists ŌĆö this contains the user's browser interactions (clicks, selections) as JSON lines 109ŌåÆ - Merge with the user's terminal text to get the full picture 110ŌåÆ - The terminal message is the primary feedback; `.events` provides structured interaction data 111ŌåÆ 112ŌåÆ4. **Iterate or advance** ŌĆö if feedback changes current screen, write a new file (e.g., `layout-v2.html`). Only move to the next question when the current step is validated. 113ŌåÆ 114ŌåÆ5. **Unload when returning to terminal** ŌĆö when the next step doesn't need the browser (e.g., a clarifying question, a tradeoff discussion), push a waiting screen to clear the stale content: 115ŌåÆ 116ŌåÆ ```html 117ŌåÆ 118ŌåÆ
119ŌåÆ

Continuing in terminal...

120ŌåÆ
121ŌåÆ ``` 122ŌåÆ 123ŌåÆ This prevents the user from staring at a resolved choice while the conversation has moved on. When the next visual question comes up, push a new content file as usual. 124ŌåÆ 125ŌåÆ6. Repeat until done. 126ŌåÆ 127ŌåÆ## Writing Content Fragments 128ŌåÆ 129ŌåÆWrite just the content that goes inside the page. The server wraps it in the frame template automatically (header, theme CSS, selection indicator, and all interactive infrastructure). 130ŌåÆ 131ŌåÆ**Minimal example:** 132ŌåÆ 133ŌåÆ```html 134ŌåÆ

Which layout works better?

file.content:
# Visual Companion Guide Browser-based visual brainstorming companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and options. ## When to Use Decide per-question, not per-session. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?** **Use the browser** when the content itself is visual: - **UI mockups** ŌĆö wireframes, layouts, navigation structures, component designs - **Architecture diagrams** ŌĆö system components, data flow, relationship maps - **Side-by-side visual comparisons** ŌĆö comparing two layouts, two color schemes, two design directions - **Design polish** ŌĆö when the question is about look and feel, spacing, visual hierarchy - **Spatial relationships** ŌĆö state machines, flowcharts, entity relationships rendered as diagrams **Use the terminal** when the content is text or tabular: - **Requirements and scope questions** ŌĆö "what does X mean?", "which features are in scope?" - **Conceptual A/B/C choices** ŌĆö picking between approaches described in words - **Tradeoff lists** ŌĆö pros/cons, comparison tables - **Technical decisions** ŌĆö API design, data modeling, architectural approach selection - **Clarifying questions** ŌĆö anything where the answer is words, not a visual preference A question *about* a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What kind of wizard do you want?" is conceptual ŌĆö use the terminal. "Which of these wizard layouts feels right?" is visual ŌĆö use the browser. ## How It Works The server watches a directory for HTML files and serves the newest one to the browser. You write HTML content, the user sees it in their browser and can click to select options. Selections are recorded to a `.events` file that you read on your next turn. **Content fragments vs full documents:** If your HTML file starts with `/.superpowers/brainstorm/` for the session directory. **Note:** Pass the project root as `--project-dir` so mockups persist in `.superpowers/brainstorm/` and survive server restarts. Without it, files go to `/tmp` and get cleaned up. Remind the user to add `.superpowers/` to `.gitignore` if it's not already there. **Launching the server by platform:** **Claude Code (macOS / Linux):** ```bash # Default mode works ŌĆö the script backgrounds the server itself scripts/start-server.sh --project-dir /path/to/project ``` **Claude Code (Windows):** ```bash # Windows auto-detects and uses foreground mode, which blocks the tool call. # Use run_in_background: true on the Bash tool call so the server survives # across conversation turns. scripts/start-server.sh --project-dir /path/to/project ``` When calling this via the Bash tool, set `run_in_background: true`. Then read `$SCREEN_DIR/.server-info` on the next turn to get the URL and port. **Codex:** ```bash # Codex reaps background processes. The script auto-detects CODEX_CI and # switches to foreground mode. Run it normally ŌĆö no extra flags needed. scripts/start-server.sh --project-dir /path/to/project ``` **Gemini CLI:** ```bash # Use --foreground and set is_background: true on your shell tool call # so the process survives across turns scripts/start-server.sh --project-dir /path/to/project --foreground ``` **Other environments:** The server must keep running in the background across conversation turns. If your environment reaps detached processes, use `--foreground` and launch the command with your platform's background execution mechanism. If the URL is unreachable from your browser (common in remote/containerized setups), bind a non-loopback host: ```bash scripts/start-server.sh \ --project-dir /path/to/project \ --host 0.0.0.0 \ --url-host localhost ``` Use `--url-host` to control what hostname is printed in the returned URL JSON. ## The Loop 1. **Check server is alive**, then **write HTML** to a new file in `screen_dir`: - Before each write, check that `$SCREEN_DIR/.server-info` exists. If it doesn't (or `.server-stopped` exists), the server has shut down ŌĆö restart it with `start-server.sh` before continuing. The server auto-exits after 30 minutes of inactivity. - Use semantic filenames: `platform.html`, `visual-style.html`, `layout.html` - **Never reuse filenames** ŌĆö each screen gets a fresh file - Use Write tool ŌĆö **never use cat/heredoc** (dumps noise into terminal) - Server automatically serves the newest file 2. **Tell user what to expect and end your turn:** - Remind them of the URL (every step, not just first) - Give a brief text summary of what's on screen (e.g., "Showing 3 layout options for the homepage") - Ask them to respond in the terminal: "Take a look and let me know what you think. Click to select an option if you'd like." 3. **On your next turn** ŌĆö after the user responds in the terminal: - Read `$SCREEN_DIR/.events` if it exists ŌĆö this contains the user's browser interactions (clicks, selections) as JSON lines - Merge with the user's terminal text to get the full picture - The terminal message is the primary feedback; `.events` provides structured interaction data 4. **Iterate or advance** ŌĆö if feedback changes current screen, write a new file (e.g., `layout-v2.html`). Only move to the next question when the current step is validated. 5. **Unload when returning to terminal** ŌĆö when the next step doesn't need the browser (e.g., a clarifying question, a tradeoff discussion), push a waiting screen to clear the stale content: ```html

Continuing in terminal...

``` This prevents the user from staring at a resolved choice while the conversation has moved on. When the next visual question comes up, push a new content file as usual. 6. Repeat until done. ## Writing Content Fragments Write just the content that goes inside the page. The server wraps it in the frame template automatically (header, theme CSS, selection indicator, and all interactive infrastructure). **Minimal example:** ```html

Which layout works better?

Consider readability and visual hierarchy

A

Single Column

Clean, focused reading experience

B

Two Column

Sidebar navigation with main content

``` That's it. No ``, no CSS, no `

Great, visual companion is live at **http://localhost:52861** ŌĆö open that in your browser. I'll use it when we get to layout/UI decisions.

Now let me make sure I understand what you're envisioning. I explored the codebase and here's what already exists:

- **Discovery carousel** ŌĆö shows trending GitHub/Docker Hub repos on the dashboard
- **Quick onboard scanner** ŌĆö paste a GitHub repo URL, it scans dependency files with LLM, lets you bulk-track them
- **GitHub OAuth** ŌĆö stores `github_id`, `github_login`, but **does not store the user's access token** (it's discarded after login)

Your idea adds two new **personalized** dimensions:
1. **Starred repos** ŌĆö show the user's GitHub stars as tracking suggestions
2. **Recently active repos** ŌĆö crawl repos the user works on and suggest their dependencies

Here's my first question:

**For the "starred repos" feature ŌĆö do you mean the user's own GitHub starred repositories (repos they've clicked the star button on), or something else?** And for the "recently working projects" ŌĆö do you mean the user's own GitHub repos (ones they own/contribute to), and then scanning those for their dependency files (like `go.mod`, `package.json`, etc.)?

message.content: yes, the user's own GitHub starred repositories and repos they own/contribute to, resue the same llm dependencies analyze

content.text:
Good, that confirms the scope. Now an important technical question.

Currently, the GitHub OAuth token is **discarded after login** ŌĆö only `read:org` scope is requested, and the token isn't stored. To fetch a user's starred repos, their own repos, and repo contents for dependency scanning, we'd need to:

1. **Request additional OAuth scopes** (at minimum `read:user` for stars, `repo` or `public_repo` for accessing repo contents)
2. **Store the GitHub access token** in the database tied to the user

**How do you feel about the scope of GitHub access?** Should we:

- **A) Public data only** ŌĆö request just enough to read the user's public stars and public repos (lower friction, less permissions)
- **B) Include private repos** ŌĆö request `repo` scope to also see private repos and their dependencies (more useful, but users may hesitate to grant broad access)

message.content: Let's start with A then in the future we will support B

content.text:
Here's a summary of decisions so far, then my proposed approaches.

## Decisions So Far

- **OAuth**: Store the GitHub token, request public-only scopes (`read:user`, `public_repo` deferred ŌĆö just public API access)
- **Placement**: Replace trending carousel with personalized suggestions for authenticated users; fall back to trending for unauthenticated
- **Trigger**: On demand ŌĆö user clicks to fetch
- **Layout**: Two tabs ŌĆö "Your Stars" and "Your Dependencies"
- **Dependencies tab flow**: Show user's repos as a checklist ŌåÆ they pick which to scan ŌåÆ reuse existing LLM dependency extraction
- **Stars tab**: Show starred repos with one-click track

## Proposed Approaches

### Approach 1: Minimal ŌĆö GitHub public API, no token storage (Recommended)

Use the **unauthenticated GitHub API** (or a single server-side `GITHUB_TOKEN`) to fetch user data by username (which we already have as `github_login`). No OAuth scope changes needed.

- `GET /users/{login}/starred` ŌĆö public stars (public repos only)
- `GET /users/{login}/repos` ŌĆö public repos

**Pros**: No OAuth changes, no token storage, no security surface. Ships fast.
**Cons**: Can't see private stars or private repos. Rate limited to 60 req/hr unauthenticated (but server-side `GITHUB_TOKEN` gives 5000/hr). Only public data.

### Approach 2: Store OAuth token, expand scopes

Change OAuth flow to request `read:user` + `public_repo` scopes. Store the encrypted token in the `users` table. Use per-user tokens for GitHub API calls.

- Full access to user's starred repos (including private stars)
- Full access to user's repos (public only with `public_repo`)
- Per-user rate limits (5000/hr per user)

**Pros**: Per-user rate limits, can see private stars, future-proof for private repo support (scope B).
**Cons**: Need to store tokens securely, handle token expiry/refresh, more complex auth flow, migration needed.

### Approach 3: Hybrid ŌĆö public API now, token storage later

Start with Approach 1 for the initial release. Build the feature around `github_login` and public APIs. Later add token storage as an opt-in "Connect GitHub" step for enhanced access.

**Pros**: Ships fastest with zero auth changes. Clean upgrade path. Separation of concerns.
**Cons**: Two implementation passes. Some users might expect private repo access.

---

**My recommendation: Approach 1 (Minimal).** Since you said public data only for now, we don't actually need to store the token at all. The user's `github_login` is already in the DB ŌĆö we can fetch their public stars and public repos using the server-side `GITHUB_TOKEN` that the onboard scanner already uses. Zero OAuth changes, zero migrations for token storage. When you're ready for private repo support later, we add token storage then.

What do you think?
Good. Let me present the design section by section.

Updated task #5 status

content.text:
## Design Section 1: Backend API

Two new endpoints under `/api/v1/suggestions/`:

**`GET /api/v1/suggestions/stars`**
- Requires authenticated session
- Fetches `GET https://api.github.com/users/{github_login}/starred` using server-side `GITHUB_TOKEN`
- Paginates (GitHub returns 30 per page, we fetch up to ~100)
- Returns list of repos with: `name`, `full_name`, `description`, `stars`, `language`, `url`, `avatar_url`
- Filters out repos already tracked as sources
- Caches per-user for 1 hour (same pattern as existing discovery cache)

**`GET /api/v1/suggestions/repos`**
- Requires authenticated session
- Fetches `GET https://api.github.com/users/{github_login}/repos?sort=pushed&per_page=100`
- Returns list of user's public repos with: `name`, `full_name`, `description`, `language`, `url`, `pushed_at`
- Used to populate the repo picker for dependency scanning

**Dependency scanning** ŌĆö reuses existing `POST /api/v1/onboard/scan` + `GET /api/v1/onboard/scans/{id}` + `POST /api/v1/onboard/scans/{id}/apply`. No new endpoints needed for the actual scanning.

No new database tables or migrations needed.

Does this section look right so far?

## Design Section 2: Frontend Flow

The personalized suggestions replace the trending carousel for authenticated users. Here's the interaction flow:

**Initial state (not yet fetched):**
- Where the trending carousel currently sits, show a card: "Discover repos from your GitHub" with two tabs: "Your Stars" | "Your Dependencies"
- Each tab has a button to fetch data ("Load your starred repos" / "Load your repos")

**Stars tab ŌĆö after fetch:**
- Grid/list of starred repos, each with: name, description, stars count, language badge
- Already-tracked repos shown with a checkmark (same pattern as existing discovery carousel)
- One-click "Track" button per repo (creates project + source, same as discovery carousel logic)
- Pagination or "Load more" if >30 results

**Dependencies tab ŌĆö multi-step flow:**
1. Click "Load your repos" ŌåÆ fetches repo list from `/api/v1/suggestions/repos`
2. Shows checkboxes list of repos sorted by last pushed. User selects which to scan.
3. Click "Scan selected" ŌåÆ calls `POST /api/v1/onboard/scan` for each selected repo
4. Shows scanning progress (reuse existing onboard progress UI)
5. Results grouped by source repo: "From `myapp` ŌåÆ express 4.18, pg 8.11, ..." with checkboxes
6. "Track Selected" button ŌåÆ calls existing `POST /api/v1/onboard/scans/{id}/apply`

**Fallback for unauthenticated users:**
- Show the existing trending discovery carousel (no change)

Does this flow make sense?

## Design Section 3: Architecture & Components

Let me show you a mockup of what this looks like.

content.text:
Check **http://localhost:52861** ŌĆö I've mocked up both tabs:

1. **Stars tab** ŌĆö grid of starred repos with Track buttons, already-tracked repos dimmed with checkmark
2. **Dependencies tab** ŌĆö repo picker with checkboxes sorted by last push, "Scan Selected" button

**Component breakdown:**
- `SuggestionsSection` ŌĆö replaces `DiscoverySection` for authenticated users. Contains tab state and delegates to child components.
- `StarsTab` ŌĆö fetches `/api/v1/suggestions/stars`, renders repo cards with track buttons. Reuses the one-click track logic from the existing discovery carousel.
- `DepsTab` ŌĆö multi-step: repo picker ŌåÆ scanning progress ŌåÆ results with track checkboxes. Reuses the existing onboard scan API and progress UI patterns.

**Backend:**
- New file `internal/api/suggestions.go` ŌĆö two handlers (`handleSuggestStars`, `handleSuggestRepos`) that proxy GitHub public API with caching.
- Registered on the existing router, auth-gated.

**No new database tables. No migrations. No OAuth changes.**

Does this architecture look right? Any changes to the mockup?

## Design Section 4: Edge Cases & Error Handling

A few things to handle:

- **GitHub rate limiting**: Server-side `GITHUB_TOKEN` gives 5000 req/hr. Cache results per-user for 1 hour. If rate limited, show a friendly "GitHub API limit reached, try again later" message.
- **User with no stars**: Show an empty state ŌĆö "You haven't starred any repos on GitHub yet" with a link to explore trending instead.
- **User with no public repos**: Same pattern ŌĆö "No public repos found" with suggestion to use the quick onboard scanner instead.
- **Scanning failures**: Individual repo scans can fail (private repo mistakenly listed as public, empty repo, no dependency files). Show per-repo status ŌĆö success/failed/no dependencies found. Don't block other scans.
- **Already tracked filtering**: Both tabs filter out repos/dependencies that are already tracked as sources. The stars tab dims them with a checkmark (shown in mockup). The dependencies results do the same.
- **Multiple concurrent scans**: The existing onboard scanner already prevents concurrent scans for the same repo (409 conflict). For multi-repo scanning, we queue them sequentially or in parallel depending on how many ŌĆö cap at 5 concurrent scans to avoid flooding.

Anything you'd add or change here?

input.content:
# Personalized GitHub Suggestions ŌĆö Design Spec

## Overview

Add personalized repo/dependency suggestions to the dashboard for GitHub-authenticated users. Replaces the generic trending discovery carousel with two tabs: "Your Stars" (user's starred repos) and "Your Dependencies" (scan user-selected repos for dependencies). Uses public GitHub API only ŌĆö no OAuth changes needed.

## Decisions

- **Data access**: Public GitHub API via server-side `GITHUB_TOKEN` (no per-user token storage, no OAuth scope changes)
- **Placement**: Replaces trending carousel for authenticated users; unauthenticated users see existing trending carousel
- **Trigger**: On demand ŌĆö user clicks to fetch each tab's data
- **Layout**: Two tabs ŌĆö "Your Stars" | "Your Dependencies"
- **Dependencies flow**: User picks repos from checklist ŌåÆ scan selected ŌåÆ review & track results
- **Dependency scanning**: Reuses existing onboard scan pipeline (LLM extraction via `POST /api/v1/onboard/scan`)

## Backend API

### `GET /api/v1/suggestions/stars`

- Auth required (session cookie)
- Fetches `GET https://api.github.com/users/{github_login}/starred` using server-side `GITHUB_TOKEN`
- Paginates up to ~100 results (GitHub returns 30/page)
- Caches per-user for 1 hour (keyed by `github_login`)
- Filters out repos already tracked as sources
- Response: array of `{ name, full_name, description, stars, language, url, avatar_url, provider: "github" }`

### `GET /api/v1/suggestions/repos`

- Auth required (session cookie)
- Fetches `GET https://api.github.com/users/{github_login}/repos?sort=pushed&per_page=100`
- Caches per-user for 1 hour
- Response: array of `{ name, full_name, description, language, url, pushed_at }`

### Dependency scanning

Reuses existing endpoints ŌĆö no new endpoints needed:
- `POST /api/v1/onboard/scan` ŌĆö start scan for a repo
- `GET /api/v1/onboard/scans/{id}` ŌĆö poll scan status
- `POST /api/v1/onboard/scans/{id}/apply` ŌĆö apply selections (create projects + sources)

## Frontend Components

### SuggestionsSection

Top-level component that replaces `DiscoverySection` for authenticated users. Manages tab state ("stars" | "deps"). Falls back to `DiscoverySection` for unauthenticated users.

### StarsTab

- Initial state: "Load your starred repos" button
- After fetch: grid of repo cards with name, description, star count, language badge
- Already-tracked repos dimmed with checkmark
- One-click "Track" button per repo (creates project + source + auto-polls, same logic as discovery carousel)
- "Show more" pagination

### DepsTab

Multi-step flow:

1. **Repo picker**: "Load your repos" button ŌåÆ fetches repo list ŌåÆ checkboxes sorted by `pushed_at` desc ŌåÆ "Scan Selected (N)" button
2. **Scanning**: Progress indicators per repo (reuses onboard progress UI patterns). Cap at 5 concurrent scans.
3. **Results**: Dependencies grouped by source repo (e.g., "From myapp ŌåÆ express, lodash, pg"). Checkboxes to select which to track. Dropdown to assign to existing project or create new one.
4. **Applied**: Success confirmation with counts (reuses onboard applied state)

## Edge Cases

- **GitHub rate limiting**: 5000 req/hr with server-side token. Cache 1hr per user. Show friendly error if rate limited.
- **No stars / no repos**: Empty state messages with suggestion to try trending discovery or quick onboard instead.
- **Scan failures**: Per-repo status (success/failed/no deps). Failed scans don't block others.
- **Already tracked**: Both tabs filter/dim repos and dependencies already tracked as sources.
- **Concurrent scans**: Cap at 5 concurrent per user. Existing 409 conflict handling for duplicate repo scans.

## Files to Create/Modify

### New files
- `internal/api/suggestions.go` ŌĆö two handlers + GitHub API client functions + caching
- `web/components/dashboard/suggestions-section.tsx` ŌĆö top-level tabbed component
- `web/components/dashboard/stars-tab.tsx` ŌĆö stars tab with repo cards
- `web/components/dashboard/deps-tab.tsx` ŌĆö dependencies tab with multi-step flow

### Modified files
- `internal/api/server.go` ŌĆö register new `/api/v1/suggestions/` routes
- `web/lib/api/client.ts` ŌĆö add `suggestions.stars()` and `suggestions.repos()` client methods
- `web/app/page.tsx` ŌĆö conditionally render `SuggestionsSection` vs `DiscoverySection` based on auth state

## Non-goals (future work)

- Private repo access (requires storing per-user OAuth tokens, expanded scopes)
- Auto-fetch on first login
- Recommendation ranking / ML-based suggestions
- Non-GitHub providers (GitLab, Bitbucket stars/repos)

Replaces the generic trending discovery carousel with two tabs: "Your Stars" (user's starred repos) and "Your Dependencies" (scan user-selected repos for dependencies). Uses public GitHub API only ŌĆö no OAuth changes needed.

## Decisions

- **Data access**: Public GitHub API via server-side `GITHUB_TOKEN` (no per-user token storage, no OAuth scope changes)
- **Placement**: Replaces trending carousel for authenticated users; unauthenticated users see existing trending carousel
- **Trigger**: On demand ŌĆö user clicks to fetch each tab's data
- **Layout**: Two tabs ŌĆö "Your Stars" | "Your Dependencies"
- **Dependencies flow**: User picks repos from checklist ŌåÆ scan selected ŌåÆ review & track results
- **Dependency scanning**: Reuses existing onboard scan pipeline (LLM extraction via `POST /api/v1/onboard/scan`)

## Backend API

### `GET /api/v1/suggestions/stars`

- Auth required (session cookie)
- Fetches `GET https://api.github.com/users/{github_login}/starred` using server-side `GITHUB_TOKEN`
- Paginates up to ~100 results (GitHub returns 30/page)
- Caches per-user for 1 hour (keyed by `github_login`)
- Filters out repos already tracked as sources
- Response: array of `{ name, full_name, description, stars, language, url, avatar_url, provider: "github" }`

### `GET /api/v1/suggestions/repos`

- Auth required (session cookie)
- Fetches `GET https://api.github.com/users/{github_login}/repos?sort=pushed&per_page=100`
- Caches per-user for 1 hour
- Response: array of `{ name, full_name, description, language, url, pushed_at }`

### Dependency scanning

Reuses existing endpoints ŌĆö no new endpoints needed:
- `POST /api/v1/onboard/scan` ŌĆö start scan for a repo
- `GET /api/v1/onboard/scans/{id}` ŌĆö poll scan status
- `POST /api/v1/onboard/scans/{id}/apply` ŌĆö apply selections (create projects + sources)

## Frontend Components

### SuggestionsSection

Top-level component that replaces `DiscoverySection` for authenticated users. Manages tab state ("stars" | "deps"). Falls back to `DiscoverySection` for unauthenticated users.

### StarsTab

- Initial state: "Load your starred repos" button
- After fetch: grid of repo cards with name, description, star count, language badge
- Already-tracked repos dimmed with checkmark
- One-click "Track" button per repo (creates project + source + auto-polls, same logic as discovery carousel)
- "Show more" pagination

### DepsTab

Multi-step flow:

1. **Repo picker**: "Load your repos" button ŌåÆ fetches repo list ŌåÆ checkboxes sorted by `pushed_at` desc ŌåÆ "Scan Selected (N)" button
2. **Scanning**: Progress indicators per repo (reuses onboard progress UI patterns). Cap at 5 concurrent scans.
3. **Results**: Dependencies grouped by source repo (e.g., "From myapp ŌåÆ express, lodash, pg"). Checkboxes to select which to track. Dropdown to assign to existing project or create new one.
4. **Applied**: Success confirmation with counts (reuses onboard applied state)

## Edge Cases

- **GitHub rate limiting**: 5000 req/hr with server-side token. Cache 1hr per user. Show friendly error if rate limited.
- **No stars / no repos**: Empty state messages with suggestion to try trending discovery or quick onboard instead.
- **Scan failures**: Per-repo status (success/failed/no deps). Failed scans don't block others.
- **Already tracked**: Both tabs filter/dim repos and dependencies already tracked as sources.
- **Concurrent scans**: Cap at 5 concurrent per user. Existing 409 conflict handling for duplicate repo scans.

## Files to Create/Modify

### New files
- `internal/api/suggestions.go` ŌĆö two handlers + GitHub API client functions + caching
- `web/components/dashboard/suggestions-section.tsx` ŌĆö top-level tabbed component
- `web/components/dashboard/stars-tab.tsx` ŌĆö stars tab with repo cards
- `web/components/dashboard/deps-tab.tsx` ŌĆö dependencies tab with multi-step flow

### Modified files
- `internal/api/server.go` ŌĆö register new `/api/v1/suggestions/` routes
- `web/lib/api/client.ts` ŌĆö add `suggestions.stars()` and `suggestions.repos()` client methods
- `web/app/page.tsx` ŌĆö conditionally render `SuggestionsSection` vs `DiscoverySection` based on auth state

## Non-goals (future work)

- Private repo access (requires storing per-user OAuth tokens, expanded scopes)
- Auto-fetch on first login
- Recommendation ranking / ML-based suggestions
- Non-GitHub providers (GitLab, Bitbucket stars/repos)

EOF
)"

     2ŌåÆ
     3ŌåÆimport (
     4ŌåÆ	"net/http"
     5ŌåÆ	"strings"

    10ŌåÆ	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/routing"
    11ŌåÆ)
    12ŌåÆ
    13ŌåÆ// Dependencies holds all external dependencies required for registering API routes.
    14ŌåÆtype Dependencies struct {
    15ŌåÆ	DB                    *pgxpool.Pool
    16ŌåÆ	ProjectsStore         ProjectsStore
    17ŌåÆ	ReleasesStore         ReleasesStore
    18ŌåÆ	SubscriptionsStore    SubscriptionsStore
    19ŌåÆ	SourcesStore          SourcesStore
    20ŌåÆ	ChannelsStore         ChannelsStore
    21ŌåÆ	ContextSourcesStore   ContextSourcesStore
    22ŌåÆ	SemanticReleasesStore SemanticReleasesStore
    23ŌåÆ	AgentStore            AgentStore
    24ŌåÆ	TodosStore            TodosStore
    25ŌåÆ	OnboardStore          OnboardStore
    26ŌåÆ	PublicURL             string
    27ŌåÆ	KeyStore              KeyStore
    28ŌåÆ	SessionValidator      SessionValidator
    29ŌåÆ	HealthChecker         HealthChecker
    30ŌåÆ	Broadcaster           *Broadcaster
    31ŌåÆ	NoAuth                bool

    35ŌåÆ
    36ŌåÆ// RegisterRoutes registers all API v1 routes on the given ServeMux.
    37ŌåÆfunc RegisterRoutes(mux *http.ServeMux, deps Dependencies) {
    38ŌåÆ	// Public chain: no auth required.

    57ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/projects/{projectId}/sources", chain(http.HandlerFunc(sources.List)))

    59ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/sources/{id}", chain(http.HandlerFunc(sources.Get)))

   143ŌåÆ	health := NewHealthHandler(deps.HealthChecker)

   145ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /api/v1/stats", chain(http.HandlerFunc(health.Stats)))

   149ŌåÆ// RegisterAuthRoutes registers OAuth and session endpoints on the mux.
   150ŌåÆ// frontendURL is used in noAuth mode to redirect back to the frontend (may be empty).
   151ŌåÆfunc RegisterAuthRoutes(mux *http.ServeMux, oauthHandler *auth.OAuthHandler, noAuth bool, frontendURL string) {
   152ŌåÆ	if noAuth {
   153ŌåÆ		mux.HandleFunc("GET /auth/me", func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
   154ŌåÆ			if c, err := r.Cookie("dev_logged_out"); err == nil && c.Value == "1" {
   155ŌåÆ				http.Error(w, `{"error":"unauthorized"}`, http.StatusUnauthorized)
   156ŌåÆ				return
   157ŌåÆ			}
   158ŌåÆ			auth.HandleMeDev(w, r)
   159ŌåÆ		})
   160ŌåÆ		mux.HandleFunc("POST /auth/logout", func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
   161ŌåÆ			http.SetCookie(w, &http.Cookie{
   162ŌåÆ				Name:  "dev_logged_out",
   163ŌåÆ				Value: "1",
   164ŌåÆ				Path:  "/",
   165ŌåÆ			})
   166ŌåÆ			w.WriteHeader(http.StatusOK)
   167ŌåÆ		})
   168ŌåÆ		mux.HandleFunc("GET /auth/github", func(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
   169ŌåÆ			// Clear the logout cookie so dev user is "logged in" again
   170ŌåÆ			http.SetCookie(w, &http.Cookie{
   171ŌåÆ				Name:   "dev_logged_out",
   172ŌåÆ				Value:  "",
   173ŌåÆ				Path:   "/",
   174ŌåÆ				MaxAge: -1,
   175ŌåÆ			})
   176ŌåÆ			target := "/"
   177ŌåÆ			if frontendURL != "" {
   178ŌåÆ				target = strings.TrimRight(frontendURL, "/") + "/"
   179ŌåÆ			}
   180ŌåÆ			http.Redirect(w, r, target, http.StatusFound)
   181ŌåÆ		})
   182ŌåÆ		return
   183ŌåÆ	}
   184ŌåÆ
   185ŌåÆ	sessionMw := oauthHandler.RequireSession
   186ŌåÆ	mux.HandleFunc("GET /auth/github", oauthHandler.HandleGitHubRedirect)
   187ŌåÆ	mux.HandleFunc("GET /auth/github/callback", oauthHandler.HandleGitHubCallback)
   188ŌåÆ	mux.Handle("GET /auth/me", sessionMw(http.HandlerFunc(oauthHandler.HandleMe)))
   189ŌåÆ	mux.HandleFunc("POST /auth/logout", oauthHandler.HandleLogout)
   190ŌåÆ}
